state_addenda_entries.override_summary: Items 1, 5, 6, 7, 11 modified to disclose the California Fast Food Act (AB 1228, Cal. Lab. Code §§ 1474-1475). Franchisee solely responsible for Fast Food Act compliance (min wage, working hours, conditions). Franchisor provides no related training, assistance, or fees. Item 7 Additional Funds estimate includes Fast Food Act cost impact effective as of the FDD issuance date.
state_addenda_entries.why_it_matters: California franchisees must absorb the entire regulatory labor-cost increase associated with AB 1228 without franchisor subsidy, training, or fee adjustment. This is unique among U.S. McDonald's markets and materially raises Item 19 pro forma downside risk for California locations.

state_addenda_entries.override_summary: Item 17 anti-waiver language: no statement, questionnaire, or acknowledgement signed at commencement can waive Hawaii Franchise Investment Law claims (including fraud in the inducement) or disclaim reliance on franchisor or franchise-seller statements. This provision supersedes other contract terms. Standard Hawaii filing disclaimer notice also included.
state_addenda_entries.why_it_matters: Hawaii franchisees retain the right to bring fraud-in-inducement and HI Franchise Investment Law claims regardless of any signed acknowledgement or questionnaire — including the 'nothing was promised' style releases that franchisors routinely rely on.

state_addenda_entries.override_summary: Item 17 modified: (1) any release required as a condition of renewal/transfer does NOT apply to liability under the Maryland Franchise Registration and Disclosure Law; (2) termination upon insolvency may be unenforceable under federal bankruptcy law (franchisor will enforce to the extent enforceable); (3) franchisee may bring MD-law claims in Maryland subject to any arbitration obligation unless preempted by the Federal Arbitration Act; (4) 3-year limitations period for MD-law claims after the grant of the franchise.
state_addenda_entries.why_it_matters: Maryland franchisees have limited but real statutory rights that survive the otherwise-strict Illinois venue + release regime. The 3-year limitations period means MD-law fraud/misrepresentation claims must be filed within 3 years of franchise grant, shorter than the 20-year term.

state_addenda_entries.override_summary: Items 13 and 17 modified. Item 13: franchisor will protect franchisees against trademark infringement claims. Item 17: nothing in FDD or FA may abrogate Minnesota Statutes Chapter 80C rights. Minn. Stat. 80C.14 Subds. 3, 4, 5 apply: 90 days' notice of termination, 60 days to cure, 180 days' notice for non-renewal, and consent to transfer shall not be unreasonably withheld.
state_addenda_entries.why_it_matters: Minnesota franchisees enjoy significantly stronger due-process protection than the default: (i) structured cure right in all terminations, (ii) 6-month non-renewal notice (double the typical), (iii) reasonableness standard on transfer denials — which is much more protective than the default 'franchisor approval required' standard in FA §15.

state_addenda_entries.override_summary: Item 17 modified. Non-compete covenants are generally considered unenforceable in North Dakota; franchisor will nonetheless seek to enforce to the extent enforceable. North Dakota law governs claims arising under the North Dakota Franchise Investment Law.
state_addenda_entries.why_it_matters: North Dakota franchisees have a substantial defense against the default 18-month / 10-mile post-term non-compete. This is the single most economically valuable state override because it restores a franchisee's right to operate any future QSR business post-term in North Dakota.

state_addenda_entries.override_summary: Item 17 modified: if any FA/FDD provisions are inconsistent with RCW 19.100.180 or the Washington Franchise Investment Protection Act (where the Act applies), the Act prevails. Separately, a Washington Assurance of Discontinuance (AOD) with the WA Attorney General requires franchisor to (1) remove no-hire/no-poach provisions from the form FA, (2) not enforce such provisions in existing FAs, (3) ask Washington franchisees to amend existing FAs to remove such provisions, and (4) notify franchisees of the AOD. No monetary penalty.
state_addenda_entries.why_it_matters: Washington is the only state with an on-record state-AG enforcement action against McDonald's no-poach/no-hire provisions. Washington franchisee employees have strongly protected mobility to other McDonald's restaurants; this also weakens the Deslandes/Turner federal no-poach cases' applicability to WA-based franchisees.

states_with_no_addendum_but_often_regula: Michigan (covered by front-matter notice, not by Exhibit T addendum)
